perturbation.original_text: 1. "Confidential Information" is that confidential, proprietary, and trade secret information being disclosed by the disclosing party pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  "Confidential Information" is that information, whether confidential, proprietary, or a trade secret, being disclosed by the disclosing party pursuant to this Agreement, as long as it is marked accordingly. If not marked, such information will not be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a requirement for marking information as 'Confidential' to qualify as such, which contradicts later sections that imply some information is inherently confidential. This could mean oral disclosures without follow up become non-confidential, introducing uncertainty. The contradiction lies in how the definition of "Confidential Information" is now conditional, whereas later sections assume all information disclosed is confidential, absent certain exceptions, regardless of whether it's marked.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Except as set forth in this Section 2, all Confidential Information shall be in tangible form and shall be marked as Confidential or proprietary information of the disclosing party. If the Confidential Information is disclosed orally or visually, it shall be identified as such at the time of disclosure and confirmed in a writing to the recipient within thirty (30) days of such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a marking requirement for confidential information, stating that if information is not marked, it won't be considered confidential. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that orally disclosed information, if identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and confirmed in writing within 30 days, is considered confidential regardless of whether it's marked. The change introduces a condition (marking) that makes the definition of 'Confidential Information' inconsistent with the procedure outlined in the contradicted section for oral disclosures, creating a strong in-text contradiction as described in the provided explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Except as set forth in this Section 2, all Confidential Information shall be in tangible form and shall be marked as Confidential or proprietary information of the disclosing party. If the Confidential Information is disclosed orally or visually, it shall be identified as such at the time of disclosure and confirmed in a writing to the recipient within thirty (30) days of such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.  Except as set forth in this Section 2, all Confidential Information shall be in tangible form. Oral or visual disclosures do not need to be confirmed in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation removes the requirement for oral disclosures to be confirmed in writing within thirty days. This contradicts the implied requirement in section 1, which now requires marking for any information to be considered confidential and it also creates an in-text contradiction with the later enforcement of confidentiality obligations, because, if the oral diclosures are not being written down for record purposes, this creates a problem in enforcing confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Each of the parties agrees that it will not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to any person, firm or business, except to the extent necessary for negotiations, discussions, and consultations with personnel or authorized representatives of the other party and any purpose the other party may hereafter authorize in writing. Each of the parties agrees that it shall disclose Confidential Information of the other party only to those of its employees, consultants, advisors and investors who need to know such information and who have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantially similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text required oral disclosures of confidential information to be confirmed in writing within 30 days. The changed text removes this requirement. Section 3 states that parties agree not to disclose confidential information and that confidential information is disclosed only to those who need to know and are bound by similar terms. The change removes the written confirmation for oral disclosures, this creates a problem for enforcing confidentiality because there is now no written record and this contradicts section 3's enforcement of obligations. Therefore, there is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Each of the parties agrees that it will not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to any person, firm or business, except to the extent necessary for negotiations, discussions, and consultations with personnel or authorized representatives of the other party and any purpose the other party may hereafter authorize in writing. Each of the parties agrees that it shall disclose Confidential Information of the other party only to those of its employees, consultants, advisors and investors who need to know such information and who have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantially similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.  Each of the parties agrees that it will not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to any person, firm or business. This restriction does not apply to information that is generally known to the public, regardless of its origin.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds an exception to the non-disclosure obligation for information 'generally known to the public,' irrespective of how it became public. This contradicts Section 4(a), which specifies that information must be in the public domain 'through no fault of the receiving party' to be exempt from confidentiality. Now, even if the receiving party caused the information to become public, it's arguably no longer confidential under Section 3, causing an internal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. There shall be no liability for disclosure or use of Confidential Information which is (a) in the public domain through no fault of the receiving party (b) rightfully received from a third party without any obligation of confidentiality, (c) rightfully known to the receiving party without any limitation on use or disclosure prior to its receipt from the disclosing party, (d) independently developed by the receiving party without use of any Confidential Information and by persons who have not had access to any Confidential Information (e) generally made available to third parties without any restriction on disclosure, or (f) communicated in response to a valid order by a court or other governmental body, as otherwise required by law, or as necessary to establish the rights of either party under this Agreement (provided that the party so disclosing has provided the other party with a reasonable opportunity to seek protective legal treatment for such Confidential Information).
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3 introduces an exception to the non-disclosure obligation for information 'generally known to the public, regardless of its origin.' This directly contradicts Section 4(a), which specifies that information must be in the public domain 'through no fault of the receiving party' to be exempt from confidentiality. The change creates a scenario where information made public due to the receiving party's actions could still be considered non-confidential under Section 3, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Each of the parties agrees that it will not modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Each of the parties agrees that it will not modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless it is technically necessary for interoperability.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the restriction on reverse engineering and decompilation, allowing it if 'technically necessary for interoperability.' This contradicts the 'unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party' clause, as interoperability needs might be present even when the disclosing party does not consent, creating an internal contradiction and uncertainty around restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Each of the parties agrees that it will not modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless it is technically necessary for interoperability.
perturbation.justification: The original text restricts reverse engineering 'unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party.' The changed text adds an exception for when it is 'technically necessary for interoperability.' This creates a contradiction because interoperability needs might arise even without the disclosing party's written consent, conflicting with the original consent requirement and creating ambiguity, therefore adhering to the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall govern all communications between the parties that are made during the period from the effective date of this Agreement to the date on which either party receives from the other written notice that subsequent communications shall not be so governed, provided, however, that each party's obligations under Sections 2 and 3 with respect to Confidential Information of the other party which it has previously received shall continue unless and until such Confidential Information falls within Section 4. Neither party shall communicate any information to the other in violation of the proprietary rights of any third party. Neither party acquires any licenses under any intellectual property rights of the other party under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement shall govern all communications between the parties that are made during the period from the effective date of this Agreement until terminated by either party with 30 days written notice. Upon termination, all obligations under this agreement cease immediately.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the termination clause. The original agreement states that even after notice is given that subsequent communications shall not be governed, the confidentiality obligations under Sections 2 and 3 continue unless the information falls under Section 4. The modified version states that upon termination, all obligations cease immediately. This contradicts the original intent that confidentiality obligations should persist for previously disclosed information even after the agreement is no longer active, thereby creating an in-text contradiction in enforcement terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. This Agreement shall govern all communications between the parties that are made during the period from the effective date of this Agreement until terminated by either party with 30 days written notice. Upon termination, all obligations under this agreement cease immediately.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that confidentiality obligations survive the termination of the agreement. The changed text and the contradicted text state that upon termination, all obligations cease immediately. This creates a direct conflict because the changed text removes the exception for confidentiality obligations, thereby contradicting the original intention to have those obligations continue even after the agreement is terminated, as outlined in the Perturbation Explanation.
